attack_types.name: Reentrancy
attack_types.description: Re-enter a function before state updates, draining funds recursively

attack_types.name: Access Control Missing
attack_types.description: Privileged functions lack onlyOwner or role checks, anyone can call

attack_types.name: Integer Overflow / Underflow
attack_types.description: Wrap uint256 boundaries to produce unintended large or zero values

attack_types.name: Unchecked Return Value
attack_types.description: transfer() or call() return value ignored, failure silently continues

attack_types.name: Delegatecall Injection
attack_types.description: Proxy delegates to attacker-controlled implementation, storage overwritten

attack_types.name: Read-Only Reentrancy
attack_types.description: View function called mid-execution while state is inconsistent across protocols

attack_types.name: Tx.Origin Phishing
attack_types.description: Contract uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender, attacker tricks user via malicious contract

attack_types.name: Self-Destruct Exploit
attack_types.description: Force-send ETH via selfdestruct breaking balance-based invariants

attack_types.name: Flash Loan Attack
attack_types.description: Borrow uncollateralized funds within single tx to manipulate state or drain liquidity

attack_types.name: Price Oracle Manipulation
attack_types.description: Skew AMM spot price used as oracle to borrow against inflated collateral

attack_types.name: Donation / Inflation Attack
attack_types.description: Donate tokens directly to vault to inflate share price, grief depositors

attack_types.name: Slippage / Deadline Missing
attack_types.description: Swap with no slippage protection, trivially sandwichable

attack_types.name: Governance Attack
attack_types.description: Acquire voting power via flash loan to pass malicious proposals

attack_types.name: Signature Replay
attack_types.description: Reuse valid signature on different chain or context without chainId/nonce check

attack_types.name: Sandwich Attack
attack_types.description: Frontrun and backrun victim tx to profit from their price impact

attack_types.name: Frontrunning
attack_types.description: Copy pending tx with higher gas to execute first and steal opportunity

attack_types.name: Cross-Chain Bridge Exploit
attack_types.description: Forge or replay messages between chains to mint unbacked tokens

attack_types.name: Private Key Compromise
attack_types.description: Leaked deployer key used to upgrade contract or drain treasury

attack_types.name: Supply Chain
attack_types.description: Malicious package mimics legitimate one, injects key exfiltration at build time

exploits.description: Compromised signing infrastructure via supply chain attack on Safe multisig UI
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attack began with a supply-chain compromise of Safe{Wallet}'s infrastructure. Attackers (Lazarus Group) compromised a Safe developer machine via social engineering/malware, gaining access to the Safe UI codebase. They injected malicious JavaScript into the app.safe.global interface (specifically targeting Bybit's cold wallet flow). The injected code was benign for all other users but, when Bybit signers loaded the UI on Feb 21, 2025, it silently altered the transaction payload. Signers believed they were approving a routine ~30,000 ETH transfer to a hot wallet; instead, the UI submitted a disguised delegatecall that overwrote the cold wallet proxy's implementation slot (storage slot 0) with an attacker-controlled contract (0xbDd077f651EBe7f7b3cE16fe5F2b025BE2969516). This single signed transaction redirected all future logic, allowing the attackers to drain 401,347 ETH (~$1.4B at the time) from the multisig cold wallet in one atomic execution. Funds were then rapidly laundered across DEXes, mixers, and bridges. The attack succeeded because the UI tampering bypassed the multisig visual verification process—signers never saw the real payload.

exploits.description: Missing access control on bridge relay function allowed unauthorized cross-chain message execution
exploits.detailed_narrative: The root cause was insufficient validation in the ReceiverAxelar contract's expressExecute-like functions. These functions were intended to process only messages originating from the Axelar network, but they lacked robust origin verification and signature checks. The attacker crafted malicious cross-chain messages that mimicked legitimate Axelar payloads. These forged messages passed the weak validation checks and instructed the ReceiverAxelar contract to release specified token amounts to attacker-controlled addresses. The ReceiverAxelar then called the PortalV2 contract to unlock liquidity, believing a valid deposit had occurred on the source chain. The attacker repeated this across every supported chain, draining liquidity pools without any corresponding source-chain deposit. After extraction, the stolen tokens were swapped into liquid assets and laundered. The team responded by shutting down bridge components and investigating. No smart-contract bug bounty or formal verification had caught the missing access-control checks on the relay path.

exploits.description: Liquidity math error from unchecked arithmetic caused inflated token minting
exploits.detailed_narrative: Truebit's Purchase contract (deployed 2021, unverified bytecode, Solidity 0.5.3) contained an unprotected addition in getPurchasePrice(uint256 amount). SafeMath guarded mul/div but not this addition, allowing overflow on absurdly large inputs. Step-by-step: (1) Attacker called getPurchasePrice() with an astronomical TRU amount → overflow returned near-zero ETH cost. (2) Minted 240+ million TRU for pennies via the mint function. (3) Approved/transferred tokens to the Purchase contract. (4) Burned them at the 12.5% buyback rate, receiving real ETH. (5) Repeated the mint-burn cycle 5 times atomically in one tx (no price impact between steps). Total: 8,535 ETH drained into Tornado Cash. The same attacker wallet had previously hit Sparkle (5 ETH). Funding traced to Rhino.fi (Nov/Dec 2025). A copycat exploited the now-public vuln for ~$250K. TRU token went to zero. The contract had no supply cap, no per-tx limits, and had sat dormant as a 'rug zone' since 2021. Attacker contract: 0x1de399967b206e446b4e9aeeb3cb0a0991bf11b8; original wallet: 0x6c8ec8f14be7c01672d31cfa5f2cefeab2562b50.

exploits.description: Oracle price manipulation allowed borrowing against inflated collateral values
exploits.detailed_narrative: YieldBlox DAO lending pool (on Blend V2, Stellar) used the Reflector VWAP oracle for USTRY/USDC collateral pricing. The market had near-zero liquidity after the only market maker withdrew. Attacker: (1) Placed a sell offer for USTRY at 501 USDC per token (100x real price ~$1.06). (2) Used a second account to buy 0.05 USTRY against that offer in one trade, dominating the oracle's 15-minute window. (3) Reflector oracle reported ~$106.74 price. (4) Protocol accepted the inflated valuation and allowed massive borrowing: 1,000,196 USDC + 61,249,278 XLM (~$10.97M total). Funds withdrawn in two blocks. $7.2M later frozen by Stellar Tier-1 validators; ~$3M unrecovered. No circuit breakers, no liquidity-depth requirements, no sanity checks on oracle inputs. Classic thin-market manipulation exploiting a configuration that routed pricing through an illiquid DEX pair.

exploits.description: Bridge message replay allowed minting unbacked wrapped tokens on destination chain
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attacker first compromised the private key of the Ethereum-side Validator contract owner. Using that key: (1) Executed a malicious upgrade to the Validator contract, stripping validation and signature checks. (2) Gained admin control over TokenSafe (reserves) and MintPool contracts. (3) Drained ~$4.3M in real tokens from TokenSafe using admin privileges. (4) Minted ~111 million unbacked CIOTX tokens via MintPool. Most minted tokens were frozen on IoTeX/Binance chains; only ~$1.7M was swapped on DEX and considered at risk. IoTeX officially valued the loss at $4.4M (discounting frozen minted assets). The upgrade bypassed all cross-chain minting safeguards, allowing arbitrary token creation without collateral on the source chain. Funds were quickly moved and laundered.

exploits.description: Deployer private key leaked, attacker upgraded program and drained treasury
exploits.detailed_narrative: The attack combined oracle deception with social engineering (not pure key leak but pre-signed admin access). Over three weeks: (1) Attacker withdrew ETH from Tornado Cash (March 11) and deployed carbonvote token (CVT) on Solana (March 12, Pyongyang-time timestamp). (2) Seeded minimal liquidity on Raydium + wash trading to peg CVT at ~$1 (oracles treated it as valid collateral). (3) Used Solana durable nonces to socially engineer Security Council members into pre-signing 'routine' admin transactions (March 23–30). (4) On March 27 Drift migrated to 2-of-5 multisig and removed timelock (zero-delay execution). (5) April 1: attacker activated pre-signed txs, listed CVT as collateral, raised limits, deposited massive fake CVT, and withdrew real assets (JLP, USDC, SOL, wBTC, wETH) in 31 txs over 12 minutes. Funds swapped via Jupiter → bridged to Ethereum → ETH/USDC/Binance. Attributed to DPRK Lazarus Group. TVL dropped ~$1B across Solana DeFi; multiple downstream protocols paused or lost funds. No code bug—pure governance + oracle manipulation via human-targeted pre-signing.

exploits.description: Price manipulation of $THE token led to bad debt on Venus Protocol
exploits.detailed_narrative: (1) Nine-month preparation phase (June 2025 – March 2026): Attacker sourced 7,447 ETH (~$16.29M) through 77 Tornado Cash transactions, deposited as collateral on Aave, borrowing ~$9.92M in stablecoins, and quietly accumulated $THE representing 84% of Venus's 14.5M $THE supply cap. (2) Bypassing the supply cap (donation attack, March 15, 2026): Used six wallets to transfer ~36M $THE directly to the vTHE contract (bypassing mint()), inflating the vTHE exchange rate 3.81×, turning a ~$3.3M position into ~$12M nominal collateral. Normal deposits added 12.2M $THE (within cap), but donations pushed total to 53.2M $THE (367% of cap). Deployed attack contract (0x737bc98f1d34e19539c074b8ad1169d5d45da619) with borrowing permissions. (3) Price manipulation and recursive borrowing loop (March 15, 2026): With inflated collateral, borrowed CAKE, BNB, BTCB, USDC, etc., using them to buy more $THE on DEXes (violent price impact due to thin liquidity). $THE price pumped from ~$0.26 to ~$4 (spot peak), but Venus's Resilient Oracle (RedStone + Binance feeds) resisted via BoundValidator for ~37 minutes. Once feeds converged at ~$0.51 (double pre-attack price), loop escalated: Borrow → Buy $THE → Donate to vTHE → Inflate rate → Borrow more → Repeat. Peak: 53.2M $THE collateral against ~$14.9M borrowed (~$30M nominal value). (4) Extraction and implosion (March 15-16, 2026): ~50 transactions extracted assets over hours. Greed led to over-leveraging; $THE crashed to $0.22 (below starting point) due to sell pressure and liquidation. Position liquidated, but thin liquidity meant $30M nominal collateral yielded nothing recoverable. Bad debt: 1.18M CAKE and 1.84M THE stuck in protocol. (5) Post-attack developments: Venus paused markets, adjusted collateral factors for illiquid assets, committed to governance proposals for bad debt resolution, supply cap hardening, and re-audits. Attacker's funds parked; potential off-chain profits via CEX shorts untraceable.
